extra.reasoning:
The property G(opted_out -> not sent) holds. Equivalently, the bad state (opted_out=true AND sent=true) is unreachable. Proof by induction on the inductive invariant I = not(opted_out AND sent):

Base case: initial state is opted_out=false, sent=false, so I holds.

Inductive step: assume I holds in the current state; check each transition cannot produce opted_out=true AND sent=true:
- t_optout (optout): sets opted_out:=true AND sent:=false simultaneously. Result has sent=false, so I holds. This is the key interaction: opting out forcibly clears sent.
- t_send_allowed (send_attempt, guard not opted_out): sets sent:=true but the guard requires opted_out=false, and opted_out is not modified, so the result has opted_out=false. I holds.
- t_send_blocked (send_attempt, guard opted_out): sets sent:=false. I holds.
- t_tick (tick): sets sent:=false (and increments ticks). I holds.

No transition ever yields opted_out=true together with sent=true: the only writer of sent:=true (t_send_allowed) is guarded by not opted_out, and the only writer of opted_out:=true (t_optout) simultaneously sets sent:=false. The other two transitions only clear sent. Since I is inductive and holds initially, it holds on every reachable state, hence on every execution (including within the 14-event bound). Property holds.
